title: Complete Ancient History – GV Sir Headshot Series
source: GvKaksha / Eduquity Pattern – SSC CGL/CHSL/MTS/Delhi Police 2026

table.sub_areas: Paleolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Metal Age, early humans
Town planning, drainage, seals, crops, sites, materials, economy
Rigveda, Samaveda, Yajurveda, Atharvaveda, Vedangas, varna, rituals
Buddha, Mahavira, Councils, doctrines, texts, symbols, places
Stupas, caves, Gandhara–Mathura art, temples, forts

subtopics.name: Correct Order of Prehistoric Periods
content: Correct order: Paleolithic → Mesolithic → Neolithic → Metal Age
PYQ Answer: B (SSC CGL)

subtopics.name: Old Stone Age / Palaeolithic Period
content: Tools: Handaxes, cleavers, and choppers
Lifestyle: Purely nomadic hunter-gatherers
Material: Quartzite stone; borers made from flakes
Fire: Traces of ash found in Kurnool caves
Ostrich egg shells found in Patne, Maharashtra
major_sites: Belum Caves – Largest & Longest cave system, Kurnool, Andhra Pradesh #imp

subtopics.name: Middle Stone Age / Mesolithic Period
content: Tools: Microliths (geometric forms – triangles, lunates, trapezes) – tiny stone tools (1–5 cm), used as arrowheads and barbed spears
Lifestyle: Nomadic to semi-sedentary
Bow and Arrow revolutionized hunting
Fishing and fowling also became important
PYQ: Mesolithic weapon used for hunting = Barbed spears (Microliths) – Ans B (SSC CGL)
major_sites: Adamgarh (M.P.) – earliest evidence for domestication of animals
Tilwara, Bagor (Rajasthan) – microlith industry & pottery introduced
Langhnaj (Gujarat) – earliest evidence of human burial
Moharana Pahara (U.P.) – earliest evidence of burial
Mahadaha & Sarai Nahar Rai (U.P.) – burials with skeletons of very tall people
Roopkund Lake, Himalayas – popularly known as Skeleton Lake

subtopics.name: New Stone Age / Neolithic Period
content: Tools: Axes called 'Celt', sickles, grinding stones (well-rounded and symmetrical shapes)
Lifestyle: Humans became food producers (farmers)
Innovations: Mud-brick houses, Cultivation, Wheel, Pottery for storing grains
Domestication of Animals
Pit dwellings (Circular/Oval) found in Burzahom and Gufkral (Kashmir – 'a cave of potters')
major_sites: Koldihwa (Uttar Pradesh) – First Neolithic site noticed in India; rice & fragmentary animal bones found #imp

Mehrgarh (Balochistan, Pakistan) #imp – Oldest Neolithic site in South Asia; River Bolan; square & rectangular houses; first evidence of cotton (7000 years ago); earliest known centre of Agriculture in South Asia
Inamgoan (Maharashtra) #imp – River Ghod; Regional Centre of Bhima valley
Piklihal (Karnataka) – People used to domesticate Cattle & ash mounds
Mahagara (U.P.) – Rice & cattle hoof marks on Clay; Cattle-Pen found #imp
Catal Huyuk – 1st Proto City, modern city of Konya, south central Turkey
Hallur (Karnataka) – Earliest Iron Age site; River Tungabhadra
Daojali Hading (Dima Hasao, Assam) – Jadeite stone, 4 types of ceramics: Cord-Marked, Incised, Stamped & plain fine red ware

subtopics.name: Metal Age
content: First metal used by early humans in ancient India: Copper (Ans: D – SSC CHSL)
Bronze = Alloy (mixture); Copper was used first
Aryans introduced Iron

subtopics.name: Overview
content: Oldest known civilization of Indian subcontinent (Ans: C – SSC CHSL)
Primary Residents: Dravidian & Mediterranean
Mesopotamia Edict: Meluha word, Haja-Bird (Peacock)
Indo-Greeks called this region: Indoi or Indike (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
First Director-General of ASI: Alexander Cunningham (established ASI in 1861)
First Indian Director-General of ASI: Daya Ram Sahni (1931–1935)
First Woman Director-General of ASI: Dr. Debala Mitra (1981–1983) – Diamond Triangle (Ratnagiri, Lalitgiri, Udayagiri), Odisha
In 1912, Harappan seals with then-unknown symbols discovered by J Fleet
Black Painted Utensils
Sweetness: Honey

subtopics.name: Characteristics of IVC
content: Rectangular Grid Pattern: Roads at 90 degree intersection
At Lothal: houses built of mud bricks; drains made of burnt bricks
Use of burnt clay bricks and ornate brick (Kali Bangan), wood & Gypsum mortar
Only Lothal city's houses doors opened towards main road; others toward backyard
City classified into Upper Part (Citadel – huge mud brick platform) and Lower Part
Unit of measurement: 16 ratio – made of stone called Chert (Cubical with no marking)
Lower denominations of weights: Binary
Higher denominations of weights: Decimal
Harappans were first in Eurasia to grow Cotton (Ans: D – SSC CHSL)
IVC was first civilization to use fire-baked bricks
All bricks same ratio: 1:2:4; Most common size 7cm high, 14cm wide, 28cm long
Bricks laid in 'header and stretcher' pattern; joined using mud mortar or gypsum for waterproofing
Wide roads of 7.2 mt run North to South and East to West; cut perpendicular
City divided into square blocks (Mohallas or Colonies)
Gates of houses opened in streets (1.8mt thick)
IVC cities enclosed with: Fortified walls with gateways (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
Standardized building material: Burnt mud-bricks (Ans: A – SSC CHSL)
Drainage system: Highly advanced and well planned (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
NOT a feature: Extensive use of iron tools (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
IVC cities had Rectangular grid pattern (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
No evidence for practice of Sati (Ans: C – SSC CGL – NOT true)

subtopics.name: Characteristics of Seals
content: A horned animal on seal of Harappa; Motif
Steatite seals used for business; Pictograph Language
God: Yoni, Phallus; Sacred: Bull, Peeple Tree
Public Hospitals: Not Available
Pashupati Mahadev Seal (Male Deity)
Citadel: Granaries, Bath, Fire Altars, Assembly / Pillared Halls, Warehouses
Main Crops: Wheat & Barley
Ornaments: Gold, Silver, Copper & Bronze
Fortified Walls & Gateways
Harappan seals made of Steatite; bear animal figures and obscure script (Ans: A – SSC CGL)
Unicorn → Single Horned → Pictographs
Seals → Trade → Commerce

subtopics.name: Mohenjodaro
content: Biggest city in IVC
Location: Sindh, Pakistan
River: Sindhu (Indus River)
Discovered: 1922; Excavated: 1925
Excavator: R.D. Banerjee (Rakhal Das Banerjee)
Drainage system notable: each house connected to a covered street drain (Ans: C – SSC CGL)

Findings: Great Bath, Granary/Warehouse, Dancing Girl (Bronze), Priest-King, Bull Seal, Pashupati Seal, Terracotta Toys & Carts, Woven Cotton Cloth, Ancient Coins, Covered Drainage System, Shell Ornaments & Weights, Wooden Doors

subtopics.name: Harappa
content: Location: Montgomery, Punjab, Pakistan
River: Ravi River
Discovered: 1921 (First site to be discovered)
Excavator: D.R. Sahini (Daya Ram Sahini)
Findings: Granaries (with circular bricks), Coffin Burial, 1st Town (sandstone statues of Human anatomy), Bullock Carts
Harappans brought Gold from Karnataka
Cemetery H and Cemetery R-37
Dancing Natraja (made of stone)
Only place having evidence of coffin burial
Harappa site discovered on banks of: Ravi River (Ans: A – SSC CHSL)

subtopics.name: Dholavira
content: Location: Kutch, Gujarat
River: Luni
Discovered: 1968 by Jagat Pati Joshi
Excavated commercially by R.S. Bisht, 1990–2005

Findings: Bullock cart driven by nude human, Water Harvesting & Reservoirs, Rocks used for construction, Drainage system, Livelihood, Wells, Bullocks, Embankments, Large cemetery with cenotaphs of 6 types, Bead processing workshops, Fallen Signboard
16 water reservoir tanks between Mansar and Manhat Stream
Divided into 3 parts: Citadel, Lower Town & Middle Town

subtopics.name: Lothal
content: Location: Ahmedabad, Gujarat
River: Bhogwa (Gulf of Khambat) – Right tributary of Sabarmati River
Discovered: 1954; Excavation: 1955
Excavator: S. Rangnath Rao
Findings: Dockyard, Bead making factory, Button seal, Fire Altars, Painted Jar, Earliest cultivation of Rice, set of Elephant Teeth, Chess Board
Drainage System made of Burnt Bricks
Biggest Dockyard of IVC; Also 1st man-made port
Manchester of IVC (cotton production)
Terracotta figures of horse and ship; jar depicting 'cunning fox' of Panchatantra
Carnelian (stone for beads) mostly found in: Gujarat (Bharuch) – Ans: B – SSC CHSL

subtopics.name: Rakhigarhi
content: Location: Hisar, Haryana
River: Ghaggar
Discovered: 1963 by Prof. Surajbhan
Excavator: Shri Amarendra Nath
Biggest/Largest Site of IVC in India (Ans: D – SSC CHSL)
Findings: Terracotta, Wheels, Toys, Figurines, Pottery
2 new sites: Tighrana & Mitathal in Bhiwani, Haryana
NOTE: Oldest Discovered site of IVC = Bhirrana (near Rakhigarhi), Fatehabad, Haryana

subtopics.name: Kalibangan
content: Location: SriGanga Nagar, Rajasthan
River: Ghaggar / Sarasvati River
Discovered: 1951 by Amlananda Ghosh
Excavator: Brij Basi Lal & Bal Krishna Thapar (1960)
Findings: Baked/Burnt Bangles, Fire Altars, Shiva Lingam, Bones of Camel, Wooden Plough
Agricultural evidence: Burnt clay plough marks in fields (Ans: B – SSC CGL)

subtopics.name: Banawali
content: Location: Fatehabad, Haryana
River: Ghaggar / Sarasvati River
Discovered: 1973
Excavator: R.S. Bisht
Findings: Barley, Terracotta Figure of plough
Suitable for observing Harappan civilization architecture

subtopics.name: Chanhudaro
content: Location: Sindh, Pakistan
River: Sindhu / Indus
Discovered: 1931
Excavator: N. Gopal Mazumdar & Mackey (Foreign)
Findings: Bead Making Factory, Use of Lipstick, Black Liner, Comb, Foot print of a dog chasing a cat
Only Harappan city without a Citadel (fortification) – Ans: C – SSC CGL
Centre for: Bead-making (Ans: C – SSC CGL)

subtopics.name: Other Important IVC Sites
content: Kot-Diji: River Indus; Excavator Fazal Ahmed Khan; 1953; Sindh (Pakistan); nearest to Mohenjo-Daro; Findings: Pottery, Stones, Arrow Heads, Bangles, metallurgy
Sutkagen Dor: Baluchistan; River Dasht; Discovered 1875 by Major Edward Mockler; Excavated 1928 by Aurelstein and George Dales; Biggest Dockyard – trade point between Harappa and Babylon (Iraq)
Daimabad (Ahmednagar, Maharashtra): On Pravara River (tributary of Godavari) – Ans: D – SSC CHSL
Manda (J&K): Chenab River

subtopics.name: Names of IVC
content: Harappan Civilization
Sindhu-Sarasvati Civilization
Indus Civilization
NOT: Mesopotamian Civilization – Ans: B – SSC CHSL

subtopics.name: Theories of Decline of IVC
content: Aryan's Theory – Moritimer Wheeler
Natural Calamity – KVR Kenedy
Ecological Imbalance – Walter Fairservis
Flooding – Marshall, Mackay & S.R. Rao
External Attack – Garden Childe
Calamatic Change – A.N. Ghosh

subtopics.name: Seven Rivers (Saptasindhu)
content: Sindhu (Indus or Singi Khamben)
Vitasta (Jhelum / Hydaspes)
Asikni (Chenab / Chandrabagha)
Parushni (Ravi / Airavati)
Vipash (Beas)
Shutudri (Sutlej / Zarodros)
Saraswati

subtopics.name: Rigvedic Period (1500–1000 BC)
content: Daughter: 'Duhitri' denoted Milker of cows
'Yava' denoted Barley
'Godhume' used denote Wheat
'Vrihi' to denote rice
Jana was the largest social unit
Egalitarian Society; No caste division
2 Assemblies: Sabha and Samiti
Holy Animal: COW (Non-Perishable Animal)
Holy River: Saraswati (Most Prominent in Rigvedic Period)
Wealthy people called Gomat; Guest: Goghana
Nishka coin (currency) used – earlier it was a throat ornament
Gavishti: Battle or Search for cows
Battle of the 10 Kings – 7th Division of Rigveda
Vedic hymns composed by: Rishis and Rishikas (Ans: D – SSC CHSL)

subtopics.name: Rigveda
content: Collection of 1028 Hymns / Suktas
Divided into 10 Mandalas #imp 2024
Oldest Religious Text; Included in UNESCO
Depicts life of Early Vedic Period
Rucha = 10600 verses
Starts with 'Agnimele Purohitam'
Two priests who played major part: Vasishtha and Vishwamitra
Contains knowledge of Chemistry & Medical Science
Rik = Sacred Hymn (Ans: C – SSC CGL)
Term for collection of hymns in Rigveda: Suktas (Ans: C – SSC CGL)
Rigveda has 1028 hymns (Ans: C – SSC CHSL)
Rigveda divided into 10 Mandalas (Ans: C – SSC CGL)
3rd Division (Mandal): Vishwamitra used Gayatri Mantra; dedicated to Savitri
8th Mandal: handwritten hymns i.e., Khil
9th Mandal: God Soma
10th Division: Purush-Sukta – discusses 4 Varnas (Brahmin, Kshatriya, Vaishya, Shudra)
Varna system belongs to later Vedic Period
Readers: Hotri (Experts of Rig Veda)
Brahmin Text: Aitareya Brahmin, Kaushitaki Brahmin
Upveda: Ayurveda
Name 'Bharata' first appears in: Rig Veda (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
Bharata Clan → Rigveda → Bharat = India

subtopics.name: Yajurveda – Book of Rituals
content: Hymns: 1975; Adhayas (chapters): 40
Contains knowledge of all Sacrificial Formulas & Weapons
Experts: Adhvaryu (Readers)
Only Veda divided into two parts: Krishna Yajur Veda (Black) & Shukla Yajur Veda (White)
UpVeda: Dhanur Veda
Specialty: both in Prose & Verse
Brahmin Text: Shatpath Brahmin, Taittiriya Brahmin
Veda with ritual formulas and sacrifices: Yajurveda (Ans: B – SSC CGL)

subtopics.name: Samaveda
content: Father of Indian Music
Hymns: 1875 (Unique Hymns: 1549); Adhayas: 6
Origin of Indian Classical Music
Sama Vedic hymns sung by priests called Udgatri
UpVeda: Gandharwa Veda
Has Mantras related to worship of Surya Dev
Contains hymns mostly taken from Rigveda, arranged for singing (Ans: A – SSC CHSL)

subtopics.name: Atharvaveda – Veda of Magical Formulas
content: Also called 'Black Magic Veda'
Hymns: 730; Khandas (chapters): 20
Collection of spells & incantations; Contains Ayurveda knowledge
Saying 'War begins in the minds of men' from Atharva Veda
Experts: Brahman
UpVeda: Shilpa Veda
Author: Atharwa Rishi
Sabha & Samiti considered two daughters of Prajapati
Blasphemy of the birth of Girls
Brahmin Text: Gopatha Brahmin
No Temple, No God → Rigvedic Age

subtopics.name: Vedangas (6 in total) #imp 2024
content: Phonetics (Pronunciation): Shiksha
Ritual (Ceremony): Kalpa
Grammar: Vyakaran
Etymology (Origin of words): Nirukta (Explanation)
Metrics: Chhanda
Astrology: Jyotisha
NOT a Vedanga: Charaka (Ans: D – SSC CGL)

subtopics.name: 6 Principal Schools of Hindu Vedic Darshan #imp
Vedic Text Categories
content: Samhitas – Collection of hymns
Brahmanas – Explain mantras and sacrificial rituals in prose form (Ans: C – SSC CHSL)
Aranyakas – Forest texts
Upanishads – Philosophical texts
Atman in Upanishads = The inner self or soul (Ans: D – SSC CHSL)
Brahdaranya Upanishad – First to give doctrine of Transmigration of Soul and Karma
Mundaka Upanishad – 'Satyameva Jayate'

subtopics.name: Varna System
content: During Vedic period, society organized into 4 Varnas (Ans: Both I and II – SSC CHSL)
4 stages of life (Ashrams) prescribed by early Hindu philosophy
Vaishya – responsible for trade and business (Ans: A – SSC CHSL)
Brahmin (Knowledge/Teaching), Kshatriya (Protection), Vaishya (Trade), Shudra (Service)

subtopics.name: Panini's Ashtadhyayi
content: Panini's grammar consists of over 4000 grammatical rules (Ans: C – SSC CHSL)
Written during Nanda Dynasty
Sanskrit Grammar text

subtopics.name: Jaherthan (Santhal Tribe)
content: Sacred grove
Religious practices of the Santhal tribe
Sacrificial rituals: Erok Sim
Inhabited by spirits and deities including Jaherera – the Lady of the Sacred Grove (guardian)
PYQ (Steno Exam Shift I – 7/08/2025): Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A (Ans: B)

subtopics.name: Life of Gautama Buddha
content: Birth: 563 BC, Lumbini, Nepal (Ans: A – SSC CHSL)
Names: Siddhartha, Gautam, Tathagat / Shakya Muni
Father: Suddhodana (Ruler of Kapilvastu)
Mother: Mahamaya Devi (Kosala Dynasty)
Foster Mother: Pajapati Gotami (1st Disciple)
Austerity Duration: 6 Years
Home sacrifice (Mahabhinishkraman): at age of 29
He was Contemporary to Bimbisara & Udayan
Nirvana: at age of 35 at Uruvela (Niranjana River), Gaya
Sambodhi: took Bodhtawa on 49th day from starting Meditation in Gaya (Bodh Gaya) – Ans: B – SSC CHSL
1st Sermon: Dharmachakkaparvatna in Sarnath near Varanasi (Dhamek Stupa built by Ashoka)
Maximum Sermon at Shravasti
Death: 483 BC, Kushinagar

subtopics.name: Five Disciples of Buddha
content: Asvajit, Upali, Mogallana, Sariputra, Ananda
NOT a disciple: Susima (Ans: D – SSC CGL)

subtopics.name: Five Great Symbols of Lord Buddha
content: Birth: Lotus, Elephant & Bull
Great Departure / Renunciation: Horse
Enlightenment (Nirvana): Bodhi Tree
First Sermon: Wheel (Noble Path / Dharm Chakra Parvartan)
Death: Stupa & Foot Print (Parinirvana)

subtopics.name: Buddhist Terminology
content: Dukkha: Suffering – 'unsatisfactoriness'
Anicca (Anitya): The doctrine of impermanence
Anatta (Anatman): The concept of 'no-self'
Pratityasamutpada: Dependent Origination – law of causality
Nirvana (Nibbana): The ultimate goal; liberation from cycle of rebirth
Samsara: The cycle of repeated birth, mundane existence, dying
Metta: Fellow Feeling (Ans: C – SSC CGL)
Arhat: A person who has attained Nirvana (ideal in Theravada)
Bodhisattva: A being who seeks enlightenment for all sentient beings (ideal in Mahayana)
Two causes of human suffering: Ignorance (Avijja) and Attachment (Tanha) – Ans: C – SSC CHSL

subtopics.name: Buddhist Texts
content: Buddhist texts written in Pali language
Jainism texts written in Prakrit

table.outcome: Vinay pitaka & Sutta Pitaka were written
1st time Buddhism divided into two sects: Sithavira & Mahasnghiska

subtopics.name: Features of Stupa
content: Medhi: Circular Terrace between railing & dome (Earth/Foundation/Path – symbolizes base of Mount Meru)
Toranas: Wall with decorated Gateways
Anda: Hemispherical Mound (Water/The Universe/Infinite Space)
Chattra: Central Pillar supporting a triple umbrella form (Air & Space / Royalty & Protection)
Harmika: A Square Railing (Fire / Abode of the Gods)
Sanchi: Hemispherical dome – Oldest Stupa (M.P.)
Kesariya Stupa: Largest Stupa in India (Bihar)
Harmika of Stupa = symbolic abode of deities at top (Ans: C – SSC CGL)
Chaityas = Congregational halls for teaching and interaction (Prayer Halls) – Ans: D – SSC CHSL
Viharas = Residence of Monks

subtopics.name: Gandhara vs Mathura Art (Comparison Table)

table.mathura: Yaksha-like, stout, fleshy, and more robust
Fine, thin, or transparent muslin drapery

table.gandhara: Often includes a moustache and beard

exam_tips: PYQ: Art form developed from fusion of Greek and Indian styles = Gandhara art (Ans: C – SSC CHSL)
Gandhara art depicted Buddha in indigenous Yaksha-like style – FALSE; that's Mathura (Ans: D – SSC CGL)
Mathura art depicted Buddha in indigenous Yaksha-like style; Gandhara showed Hellenistic features
Chronological order of Buddhist sculpture: Sanchi Stupa-1 → Bharhut → Mathura (Answer: c-b-a = C – SSC CGL)
Sanchi & Bharhut = Symbolic (no human Buddha); Mathura & Gandhara = Human figures
Both Gandhara & Mathura patronized by Kushanas (Kanishka)
Gandhara credited with FIRST sculptural representation of Buddha in human form
Earlier Indian art (like Sanchi) was aniconic – used symbols like footprints or lotus
Kankalitila site (Mathura) – Famous for early Jain sculptures (Tirthankaras)
Royal Statues: Headless statue of Kanishka found at Mathura
Mathura art reached pinnacle during Gupta Period – transparent drapery
subtopics.amaravati_tip: If question mentions White Marble → answer is almost always Amaravati School (South India, Krishna-Godavari valley, patronized by Satavahanas)

subtopics.name: Architectural Styles Comparison
content: Indo-Islamic Style features: domes, minarets, arches (pointed but without flying buttresses), marble or red sandstone; NO ribbed vaults, flying buttresses, or stained glass
South India (Deccan/Dravidian) Style: vimanas (temple spires), gopurams (monumental gateways), pillared mandapas
Gothic (Victorian) Architecture: Medieval European (12th–16th century); Pointed Arches, Ribbed Vaults, Flying Buttresses, Stained Glass Windows

Gothic buildings in India: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus (Mumbai, F.W. Stevens), St. Paul's Cathedral (Kolkata), Rajabai Clock Tower & Mumbai University (Sir George Gilbert Scott), St. Philomena's Church (Mysuru), High Court of Bombay
Romanesque: Rounded (Semi-circular), Thick, heavy, load-bearing, Small, narrow, dark, Massive internal columns
PYQ: Architecture with ribbed vaults, pointed arches, flying buttresses, stained glass = Gothic (Ans: D – Steno Exam)

subtopics.name: Golden Age of Indian Temple Architecture
content: Gupta Period = Golden age of Indian temple architecture (Ans: A – SSC CHSL)

subtopics.name: Mahajanapadas Overview
content: Vajji, Malla & Assaka (Ashmaka) were Republics; rest all were Monarchy
'Bhaga' was the tax collected on crops at the rate of 1/6th
Magadh (most powerful) surrounded by river Ganga & Son
Anguttar Nikaya (Buddhist Scripture) describes 16 Mahajanapadas
Second urbanization in Ganga valley: 6th century BCE
First Dynasty to rule Magadh: Haryanka Dynasty

table.location: District Patna, Gaya & Part of Shahabad in Bihar
Faizabad, Gonda Region or Eastern U.P.
South of Vaishali distt. in U.P.
Punch area in Kashmir & Hazara District of Pakistan
Dist. of Allahabad Mirzapur of U.P.

subtopics.name: Anga Mahajanapada
content: Capital at Champa (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
Located in Munger & Bhagalpur district of Bihar

subtopics.name: Taxila
content: Famous centre of learning
Capital of Gandhara Mahajanapada (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)

subtopics.name: Haryanka Dynasty (544–413 BC)
content: Founder/1st Ruler: Bimbisara (son of Bhattiya) (544–491 BC)
Also called: Sreniya / Seniya
Capital (Magadh): Grivraja / Rajgirha
Contemporary of Buddha; Jain literature: Shrenika/Seniya
Three wives: Mahakosala, Chellana & Kshema
Mahakosala = Sister of Kosal ruler Prasenjit; got Kasi as dowry
Chellana = Lichchhavi Princess; got Vriji (Vaishali) region
He defeated Anga & annexed it
Sent Physician Jivak to treat Chand Pradyot of Avanti
Prisoned by son Ajatshatru; died in 493 BC
Ajatshatru (2nd ruler) – held 1st Buddhist Council at Rajagriha in 483 BC

subtopics.name: Shishunaga Dynasty (413–343 BC)
content: Founder/1st Ruler: Shishu Naga (Minister of Naga dasak; killed Naga dasak)
413–395 BC; Shifted capital to Vaishali #imp
Won Avanti Area to Magadha
2nd Ruler: Kalashoka (son of Shishu Naga)
2nd Buddhist Council held at Vaishali under Kalashoka #imp
Other Name of Kalashoka: KAKAVARIN
395–367 BC; Shifted capital to Patliputra #imp
Last Ruler: Mahanandin

subtopics.name: Mauryan Dynasty Overview
content: Chandragupta Maurya established Mauryan Empire in 321 BC (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
Arthashastra written by Chanakya / Kautilya (guide to statecraft)

subtopics.name: Ashoka (3rd Ruler) – 268–232 BC
content: Dev Nama Piya (Beloved of Gods)
Built the Sanchi Stupa (Madhya Pradesh)
Sent son Mehendra and daughter Sanghmitra to Sri Lanka (Ceylon) and Lakshadweep for propagation of Buddhism
Southernmost side-city in Mauryan Empire: Brahmagiri
Kalinga War: 261 BCE; Dhauli elephant sculpture associated with Kalinga War – symbolizes Ashoka's transformation
Dhauli elephant = one of earliest examples of rock-cut animal sculpture in India
Mauryan architecture advanced most during Ashoka's reign (Ans: C – SSC CHSL)

subtopics.name: Mauryan Administration
content: 18 Tirthas
Amatyas: Civil servants for day-to-day administration
Rajukas: Responsible for clerical & accounting; could give capital punishment
Patliputra (capital) administered by 6 Committees, each with 5 Members
Kantaka Sodhana: Court of criminal justice
5 Provincial Centres of Magadh: 1) Taxila (Trade Route), 2) Ujjayani (Trade Route), 3) Tosali, 4) Suvarnagiri (Gold trade with Karnataka), 5) Patliputra (Main Capital)

subtopics.name: Ashokan Pillar Sites
content: Lauriya Nandangarh – Bihar (Correctly matched Ans: A – SSC CGL)
Sarnath – Uttar Pradesh (NOT Gujarat)
Sanchi – Madhya Pradesh (NOT Odisha)
Amaravati – Andhra Pradesh (NOT M.P.)

subtopics.name: Kanishka (Kushana Dynasty, 78–144 AD)
content: Also known as 2nd Ashoka
Started Saka Era (78 AD)
Title: Devputra, 'Shah Naushahi'
2 Capitals: Peshawar (1st) & Mathura (2nd)
Silk route in central Asia
Last great Kushan ruler: Vasudeva I
Bamiyan: Standing Buddhist Statue
Scholars in Kanishka's court:
- Charaka (Rajvaidya) – Charak Samhita
- Ashwaghosh – Buddhacharitra (Ramayana of Buddhism) & Saundarananda (Sanskrit)
- Vasumitra – Mahaavibhaash Sootra; Head Priest – Confederacy
- Nagarjuna (Einstein of India by Chinese traveler Xuanzang / Hiuen Tsang)
- Vatsyayan – Kamasutra
4th Buddhist Council held under Kanishka in Kashmir

subtopics.name: Post-Mauryan Buddhist Stupas
content: Post-Mauryan Buddhist stupas clearly show regional variations in architectural form
Cruciform bases (seen in Gandhara region)
Pyramidal or tiered superstructures (north-western regions)
Differences in dome shape, harmika, and railings across regions
Variations due to locally available materials, regional construction techniques, and local cultures / foreign contacts

subtopics.name: Udayagiri & Khandagiri Caves
content: Near Bhubaneswar – meant for Jain monks (Ans: A – Steno Exam)
Not Buddhist temples

subtopics.name: Gupta Dynasty Overview
content: Founder/1st Ruler: Sri Gupta (Vaishya Clan), 240–280 AD; Title: Maharaja; Feudatories of Kushanas
3rd Ruler: Chandragupta I (319–330 AD) – Real founder of Gupta Empire; son of Ghatotakacha
Enhanced power by marrying Kumara Devi (Princess of Lachchavi Clan of Nepal)
Their son: Samudra Gupta
Title of CG I: 'Maharajadhiraj & Dhiraja'
Ruled over Magadh, Saket & Prayag; Empire: Bihar, Uttar Pradesh and Bengal

subtopics.name: Samudra Gupta (4th Ruler, 330–380 AD)
content: Greatest Ruler of Gupta Empire (Prakramana)
Napoleon of India (by historian V.A. Smith) – Ans: B – SSC CHSL
Title: 'Kaviraj & Vikramanka'
Interested in Music; issued coins showing him playing Vina
Achievements mentioned in Prayag-Prashasti composed by Harisena
Prayag Prashasti = Allahabad Pillar Inscriptions (Eran Inscriptions, M.P.)
Harisena and Vasubandhu adorned his court; Harisena wrote Brihat Katha Kosh
He defeated 12 rulers of Dakshinapatha
Ashwamedha Yajna & Yupa type Gold Coins
Harisena's offices: 1) Mahadandanayaka (Chief Judicial Officer), 2) Kumar Amatya (important Minister), 3) Sandhi-Vigrahika (Minister of war and peace)
Composer of Allahabad Pillar Inscription: Harisena (Ans: C – SSC CHSL)
Primary literary source of Gupta period: Allahabad Pillar inscription (Ans: C – SSC CGL)

subtopics.name: Chandragupta II / Vikramaditya (5th Ruler, 380–415 AD)
content: 1st Gupta to issue silver coins named 'Rupaka'; period called Golden Period #imp
Court at Ujjain
Got title of 'Vikramaditya' by defeating Rudrasimha-3 (Kshatrap king of Ujjain) #imp
Iron Pillar Inscription (near Qutab Minar, Delhi) mentions king Chandra #imp
Court adorned by celebrated Nine Gems (Navaratnas) including Kalidasa #imp
Chinese Pilgrim 'Fahien' visited India during his reign #imp 2024
Married princess Kubernaga (Naga Alliance); daughter Prabhavati
Married daughter to Rudrasena-2 of Vakataka Dynasty #imp 2024
Defeated Saka king Rudrasimha III → title: SAKARI
Mehrauli Iron Pillar inscription: authority over NW India and part of Bengal #imp
Virasena's Udaigiri Cave inscription: conquest of the whole world
Made Ujjain his second capital
Veerasena Saba = court poet and minister of Chandragupta II #imp
Amarkhaddava = Army General
Issued silver and copper coins; predecessors issued only gold coins
Gold coins called 'Dinara'

subtopics.name: Kumar Gupta I (6th Ruler, 415–455 AD)
content: Founded Nalanda University (Bihar)
Fought with 'Hunas' & died during war
Title: Shakraditya, Mahendraditya

subtopics.name: Skanda Gupta (7th Ruler, 455–467 AD)
content: Title: Kramaditya, Shakropam
Defeated Hunas 2 times
Repaired Sudarshan Lake
Last known ruler: VishnuGupta (540–550 AD)

subtopics.name: Gupta Era Fiscal Terms
content: Bhaga: King's share of produce (1/6th) – Ans: C – SSC CGL
Visti / Veti: Forced/corvée labour – Ans: D – SSC CGL
Hiranya: Cash tax (NOT compulsory labour service)
Dasaparadha: Fines from ten offences
Bhoga: Free-labour levy
Adeya: Registry-keeping duty

subtopics.name: Gupta Administration Officials
content: Utkhetayita: Tax/dues collector from public
Dhruvdhikaranika: Collects revenue (Bhaga) in grain; 'Dhruv' = certainty of revenue
Pustapala: Record Keeper / Registrar – land details, boundaries, ownership
Bhandagaradhikrita: Head of Royal Treasury / Warehouse
Prathamakulika: Chief Craftsman / Head of Craftsmen's Guild
Gramik: Village Head – lowest administrative officer
Talavatka: Lower-level military/police officer

subtopics.name: Palas of Bengal (Capital – Monghyr)
content: 1st Ruler: Gopala (750–770 AD)
2nd Ruler: Dharmapala (770–810 AD) – son of Gopala
Dharmapala founded Vikramsila University and revived Nalanda University
Built Somapuri Monastery in Bihar
Fought battles against Pratiharas and Rashtrakutas in Tripartite Struggle
Imperial titles: Paramabhattaraka, Paramesvara & Maharajadhiraja
Devapala – 3rd Ruler; Court Poet: Buddhist Scholar Vajra datta (author of Lokesvarasataka)
Syadvada: Philosophical system developed by Lord Mahavira (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)

subtopics.name: French Colonial Architecture
content: Blend of European design principles adapted to Indian climate and local influences
Symmetry and balance
Wide verandas or galleries for shade and ventilation in tropical climates
Tall doors and windows for natural light and cross-ventilation
Sloped or pitched tiled roofs for efficient rainwater drainage in monsoons
Raised basements to protect from flooding
Minimal ornamentation in early stages; later incorporated local decorative elements or wrought iron
Columns and porticos reflecting classical European architecture

subtopics.name: Key Facts – Jainism
content: Lord Mahavira developed Syadvada philosophical system (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)
Syadvada = Doctrine of 'perhaps' / conditional predication
Jain texts written in Prakrit language

subtopics.name: Key Facts
content: River: Tigris and Euphrates (now part of Iraq)
Known for: Astronomy, Mathematics and Literature
Very Prosperous; Language: Sumerian
Later gradually replaced by the Akkadian Civilization
They used to write on tablets made of Clay #imp
NOT a name for Indus-Sarasvati Civilization (Ans: B – SSC CHSL)

pyqs.question: Term 'Rik' in Rigveda = Sacred Hymn
Correct order of prehistoric periods = Paleolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Metal Age
Key difference between Mathura and Gandhara art – Mathura depicted Buddha in indigenous Yaksha style; Gandhara reflected Hellenistic (Greco-Roman) features
NOT true about Harappan culture – There is clear evidence for practice of Sati
Buddhist Councils and locations – First Buddhist Council: Rajagriha
Term for collection of hymns in Rigveda = Suktas
NOT a Vedanga = Charaka
Gupta fiscal term – Visti = forced or corvée labour
Chronological order of Buddhist sculpture = c-b-a (Sanchi Stupa-1 → Bharhut → Mathura)
Ashokan pillar – Lauriya Nandangarh is in Bihar
Drainage system at Mohenjodaro – each house connected to a covered street drain
Gupta era obligations – Veti = forced labour
Metta in Buddhism = Fellow feeling
Agricultural evidence at Kalibangan = Burnt clay plough marks in fields
Chanhudaro primarily centre for = Bead-making
Mesolithic weapons for hunting = Barbed spears (Microliths)
NOT a disciple of Buddha = Susima
Architecture with ribbed vaults, pointed arches, flying buttresses, stained glass = Gothic
Oldest known civilisation of Indian subcontinent = Indus Valley Civilization
Circular brick platforms at Harappa used for religious ceremonies – A is false but R is true
Jaherthan plays a central role in sacrificial rituals of Arok Sim – Both A and R correct
Udayagiri and Khandagiri – near Bhubaneswar, meant for Jain monks = Only 1
Primary literary source of Gupta period = Allahabad Pillar inscription of Samudragupta
Rigveda divided into = 10 Mandalas
Harmika of a stupa = symbolic abode of deities at the top; Statement II (relics kept there) is false
Harappan seals – made using steatite; bear animal figures and obscure script = Both 1 and 2
Veda with ritual formulas and sacrifices = Yajurveda
Panini's grammar = over 4000 grammatical rules
NOT a feature of Indus Valley cities = Extensive use of iron tools
Veda with hymns arranged for singing = Samaveda
Atman in Upanishads = inner self or soul
IVC cities enclosed with = Fortified walls with gateways
Golden age of Indian temple architecture = Gupta Period
Harappan cities had ____ grid pattern = Rectangular
Varna responsible for trade and business = Vaishya
IVC used standardized ____ as building material = Burnt mud-bricks
Second Buddhist Council held during reign of = Kalashoka
Vedic text category explaining mantras in prose = Brahmanas
Rik signifies = Sacred Hymn
First metal used by early humans in India = Copper
Mahajanapada Anga capital in Bihar = Champa
Siddhartha Gautam meditated for many days at = Bodh Gaya
Harappa discovered on banks of = Ravi River
Main function of Chaityas = Congregational halls for teaching and interaction
Taxila was capital of = Gandhara Mahajanapada
Harappans first in Eurasia to grow = Cotton
Name Bharata first appears in = Rig Veda
Vedic hymns composed by = Rishis and Rishikas
Vedic period society in 4 varnas + 4 stages of life = Both I and II
Rig Veda collection of approximately how many hymns = 1028
Art form from fusion of Greek and Indian = Gandhara art
Approximate year of birth of Gautama Buddha = 560 BCE
Harappan drainage system = Highly advanced and well planned
Two causes of human suffering = Ignorance and Attachment
NOT a name for Indus-Sarasvati Civilization = Mesopotamian Civilization
Chandragupta Maurya established Mauryan Empire in = 321 BC
Philosophical system of Lord Mahavira = Syadvada
Rakhigarhi located in = Haryana
Carnelian stone mostly found in = Gujarat
Daimabad Harappan site located in = Maharashtra
Composer of Samudragupta's Allahabad Pillar Inscription = Harisena
Ancient Greeks named Indus River region = Indoi or Indike
Napoleon of India = Samudragupta
